speaker: interviewer
text: And we're going to listen to Mr. Eisenberg talk about his pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was-- taken I don't know how old I was. This was taken in our hometown, Sasow. I'm the-- I'm the one who's holding the horse. And this is me. And then my youngest brother is the first one. His name was Aaron. And the-- then the-- this-- the other brother is in between me and him. I mean, the middle his name was Hershel. And what-- this picture is from my wife's family. It was taken in 1938. And my wife is that little one, that cute, the-- the smallest one, the one with the white dress. At that time, she never met me at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Miriam [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Miriam [INAUDIBLE]. You know in Jewish, [INAUDIBLE] means miracles. A miracle she married me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in Tyszowce.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland, sure. Which one's me? I think I'm the one on the end. This picture was taken in the Italian border. We were bringing from-- while I was in Bricha. And this was in Brenner Pass, it's called. And I'm the one right in here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us approximately where on the-- on the photograph you're located?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the first-- the second one from the right, with the uniform, the good-looking guy. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture was from Bricha, in Salzburg. And this is the type we used to use. We-- we were using all kind of trucks and cars. But this was-- and later we-- we put then, a Jewish star on it. And it was taken in Salzburg. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture is taken in Salzburg, in the railroad station. I'm the one with the outstretched hand, directing the people to go in to-- to the trains. I'm the first one with the hand out. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture's also from Bricha. And these are the trucks that were loading and unloading the people going different places. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture is taken for a few of our workers in Bricha. I'm the one-- the second one from the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which-- which row?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- in the top row, in the second row, whatever you call. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture, the most important picture, me and my wife and my daughter's dog, which her name is Molly. Beautiful dog, very good dog.

speaker: interviewer
text: --have to sit back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sit back.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture from my-- part of my family. Me with the blue sweater, my daughter, Rosalie, my wife, Miriam, my son-in-law, Maury, my granddaughter, Samantha. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] These are my three precious grandchildren, from right to left, with the dark suit, that's Brian, studying to be a doctor. Michael, which is a genius, a whiz with computers, and Samantha, very smart. This is Brian, Michael, and Samantha. This picture from my whole family. My son, Norman, from right to left. My son, Norman. Next to Norman is me. This is my beautiful wife, Miriam. My grandson, Michael, my daughter, Rosalie, my son-in-law, Maury, my other grandson, Brian. And then the middle one is Samantha.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Eisenberg, could you please tell me who all is sitting on the couch with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. My right side, my grandson, Michael. I'm next to him. Next to me is my lovely wife, Miriam. My granddaughter, Samantha, my daughter, Rosalie, and my son-in-law, Maury.

speaker: interviewer
text: ROSALIE (DAUGHTER): Can we also say who's missing? Yes. Who is-- who is missing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, one-- one grandson is missing. He is in college, in Brandeis college. He is-- he could not attend. And my son is also missing. My son, Norman, he couldn't make it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Eisenberg, would you like to say something to your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: MIRIAM (WIFE): I'll say a few words. He was so nice. I was very proud of him. You know, he was really doing a good job. And I just want to thank him for all the words he had for me. So good luck, Georgie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rosalie, would you like to say something to your dad? ROSALIE (DAUGHTER): Yes, I would. I feel very lucky, having grown up in a house where my father always very openly spoke about his Holocaust experiences. And because of that, being Jewish has been a very integral part of my life.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what he's doing by documenting this, I think, is so important. I know that years ago, when my son was in middle school, my father came and spoke to Michael's class. And I was amazed to realize that for some of those children in the class, it was the first time they'd ever heard of the Holocaust. Here were 11, 12, 13-year-old kids that had never even heard of the Holocaust. And my father came in. And you know, that's a difficult age to hold anyone's attention. And he was speaking for about an hour. And you could have heard a pin drop. And so I realized how important what he's doing in documenting this is. And I'm really proud of him. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you all for being here. I'm sure you look forward to seeing his tape. And I thank you, especially Mr. Eisenberg, for all your wonderful information.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome. MIRIAM (WIFE): It was a pleasure. Right. MIRIAM (WIFE): Just meeting you both was-- [LAUGHTER] --it was our pleasure.
